source_paper_title: Lipschitz Stability of Least-Squares Problems Regularized by Functions with C2-Cone Reducible Conjugates

context:
Page 19 (Conclusion), paragraph beginning 'One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future...'; also reiterated on page 20.

One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. ... Finding the Lipschitz modulus without relying on second-order information of \(g\), as in our approach, remains an open area of research that we wish to continue exploring.
title: Compute Lipschitz modulus of regularized least-squares solution mapping under stability
background_latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces, \(A\in \mathcal{L}(X,Y)\) a linear operator with adjoint \(A^*\), \(b\in Y\), and \(\mu>0\). Let \(g:X\to \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed, proper, and convex. Consider the regularized least-squares problem
\[
\min_{x\in X}\; F_{A,b,\mu}(x):=\frac{1}{2\mu}\|Ax-b\|^2+g(x),
\]
with solution mapping
\[
S(A,b,\mu):=\operatorname{argmin}_{x\in X} F_{A,b,\mu}(x).
\]
Assume that around a reference parameter triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) the mapping \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz, i.e., there exist neighborhoods \(\mathcal{U}\) of \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) and a constant \(L\ge 0\) such that
\[
\|S(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-S(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\le L\,\|(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\quad \forall (A_i,b_i,\mu_i)\in \mathcal{U}.
\]
The infimum of all such \(L\) (for a fixed choice of local neighborhoods) is called the (local) Lipschitz modulus of \(S\) at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\); informally, it is the smallest constant governing the local Lipschitz bound.

The paper establishes first-order characterizations for when this Lipschitz stability holds, but notes that computing the Lipschitz modulus in general can be approached via coderivatives (which typically require second-order-type variational information about \(g\)).
problem_definition_latex: Determine (or provide computable formulas/estimates for) the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S\) at a reference triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) in the regime where \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz around \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\), using an approach that does not rely on involved second-order variational structures of the regularizer \(g\) (e.g., avoiding explicit computation of second-order objects such as coderivatives of \(\partial g\) that are difficult beyond polyhedral cases).
latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces, \(A\in \mathcal{L}(X,Y)\) a linear operator with adjoint \(A^*\), \(b\in Y\), and \(\mu>0\). Let \(g:X\to \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}\) be closed, proper, and convex. Consider the regularized least-squares problem
\[

\]
with solution mapping
\[

\]
Assume that around a reference parameter triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) the mapping \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz, i.e., there exist neighborhoods \(\mathcal{U}\) of \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) and a constant \(L\ge 0\) such that
\[
\|S(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-S(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\le L\,\|(A_1,b_1,\mu_1)-(A_2,b_2,\mu_2)\|\quad \forall (A_i,b_i,\mu_i)\in \mathcal{U}.
\]

Determine (or provide computable formulas/estimates for) the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S\) at a reference triple \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) in the regime where \(S\) is single-valued and locally Lipschitz around \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\), using an approach that does not rely on involved second-order variational structures of the regularizer \(g\) (e.g., avoiding explicit computation of second-order objects such as coderivatives of \(\partial g\) that are difficult beyond polyhedral cases).

open_problem_quote: One of the open questions we plan to investigate in the future is the computation of the Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping when Lipschitz stability occurs. ... Finding the Lipschitz modulus without relying on second-order information of \(g\), as in our approach, remains an open area of research that we wish to continue exploring.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The task is to derive general formulas or computable bounds for a modulus, not to refute a universal claim. While one can test candidate bounds on instances, disproving or validating a proposed general expression would still require analytic arguments beyond finite counterexample search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could propose and empirically evaluate candidate upper/lower bounds or estimators of the modulus on families of computable instances (e.g., specific choices of \(g\) and random \(A,b\)) by numerically estimating local Lipschitz constants. However, translating empirical patterns into general, provable formulas is nontrivial, so computational search is mainly useful for conjecturing sharp constants or discovering structure in special classes.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The 2024 source paper (Cui–Hoheisel–Nghia–Sun) reframes the regularized least-squares KKT system through the Fenchel–Rockafellar dual and applies Robinson strong regularity to obtain a purely first-order characterization of when the solution mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) is locally single-valued and Lipschitz: under \(C^2\)-cone reducibility of \(g^*\) at \(\bar z=-\tfrac{1}{\bar\mu}\bar A^*(\bar A\bar x-\bar b)\), Lipschitz stability is equivalent to the subspace qualification \(\ker \bar A\cap \operatorname{par}\partial g^*(\bar z)=\{0\}\). The paper explicitly flags as open the quantitative step of computing the local Lipschitz modulus (the smallest Lipschitz constant) without resorting to coderivatives/second-order variational objects for \(g\).

Forward-citing work so far provides partial progress mainly in polyhedral settings. The survey/talk “Stability of nonsmooth optimization problems” retains coderivative-based criteria in general but, for LASSO, derives an explicit computable upper bound on the Lipschitz modulus in terms of singular values of an active submatrix \(A_J\) and the scaled residual \(A\bar x-\bar b\). “Lipschitz continuity of solution multifunctions of extended regularization problems” gives polyhedral-geometry characterizations (global Lipschitzness in \((\lambda,b)\) for fixed \(A\), and local Lipschitzness under an active-set linear independence condition) but does not provide an explicit modulus. “Isolated Calmness in Regularized Convex Optimization” supplies geometric necessary/sufficient conditions for isolated calmness in broader composite least-squares models, which supports existence of some local Lipschitz bound but again stops short of a computable minimal constant.

Overall, the problem of determining the exact local Lipschitz modulus of \(S\) at \((\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu)\) in the general \(C^2\)-cone reducible-conjugate regime, using only first-order information about \(g\), remains open. Current promising directions include: (i) exploiting active-manifold/effective-subspace reductions (as in Newton methods for polyhedral regularizers) to express the modulus via restricted linear systems; and (ii) extending such reductions beyond polyhedral \(g\) to broader \(C^2\)-cone reducible \(g^*\) by identifying computable representations of \(\operatorname{par}\partial g^*(\bar z)\) and deriving tight norm bounds for the implicit map induced by the dual strong-regularity linearization.

sources.title: Stability of nonsmooth optimization problems
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the regularized least-squares problem \(\min_x \tfrac12\|Ax-b\|^2+\lambda g(x)\) with \(g\) closed proper convex, the talk derives a Mordukhovich-type sufficient condition for local single-valued Lipschitz stability of the solution map \((A,b,\lambda)\mapsto \arg\min_x \tfrac12\|Ax-b\|^2+\lambda g(x)\): if \(\bar x\) solves the problem and \(\bar u:=\tfrac1\lambda A^*(b-A\bar x)\in\partial g(\bar x)\), then the Mordukhovich criterion holds provided \[\ker A\cap D^*(\partial g^*)(\bar u\mid \bar x)(0)=\{0\}.\]
In the polyhedral-support case \(g=\sigma_P\), this reduces to the computable qualification \(\operatorname{par}\partial g^*(\bar u)\cap\ker A=\{0\}\) (denoted (R)). For unconstrained LASSO \(g(x)=\|x\|_1\), under the intermediate condition \(\ker A_J=\{0\}\) (equivalent to (R)), the solution map \(S(b,\lambda)\) is semismooth and locally Lipschitz at \((\bar b,\bar\lambda)\) with an explicit upper bound on the Lipschitz modulus:
\[L\le \frac{1}{\sigma_{\min}(A_J)^2}\Big(\sigma_{\max}(A_J)+\big\|A_J^T(A\bar x-\bar b)/\bar\lambda\big\|\Big).\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computable formulas/estimates of the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) for regularized least squares, preferably avoiding heavy second-order objects (coderivatives of \(\partial g\)). This citing work does not give a general formula for the exact Lipschitz modulus for arbitrary convex \(g\); it still uses the Mordukhovich criterion and coderivative expressions to certify Lipschitz stability. However, it provides (i) a reduction of the stability condition to a more tangible qualification \(\operatorname{par}\partial g^*(\bar u)\cap\ker A=\{0\}\) in polyhedral-support/PLQ settings, and (ii) an explicit, easily computable upper bound on the Lipschitz modulus for the LASSO solution map in terms of singular values of a submatrix \(A_J\) and the residual \(A\bar x-\bar b\). Thus it constitutes partial progress toward computable modulus estimates in important special cases (\(\ell_1\), PLQ, \(C^2\)-cone reducible conjugates), but does not resolve the general-modulus computation problem posed in the original paper.

sources.title: Lipschitz continuity of solution multifunctions of extended regularization problems
sources.theorem_or_result: For the extended \(\ell_1\) regularization/Lasso solution multifunction \(S(\lambda,b,A)\), the paper proves: (a) for fixed \(A\), \(S(\cdot,\cdot,A)\) is globally Lipschitz continuous on its convex domain \(\mathbb R_+\times\mathbb R^m\) (Theorem 3.1) via a polyhedral decomposition of \(\operatorname{gph}S\); (b) for \(\lambda>0\), local Lipschitz continuity of \(S\) around \((\bar\lambda,\bar b,\bar A)\) is equivalent to Condition 1.2 (linear independence of active columns) (Proposition 4.1); and (c) on compact \(U\subset \mathbb R_{++}\times\mathbb R^m\), \(S\) is Lipschitz on \(U\times V\) for some neighborhood \(V\) of \(\bar A\) iff Condition 1.2 holds at \((\lambda,b,\bar A)\) for all \((\lambda,b)\in U\) (Theorem 4.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computable formulas/estimates of the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping for general convex regularizers \(g\), avoiding coderivatives/second-order variational objects. This paper addresses a special polyhedral regularizer \(g(x)=\|x\|_1\) (Lasso/extended \(\ell_1\) model) and establishes Lipschitz continuity characterizations using polyhedral theory rather than coderivatives. However, it does not compute the Lipschitz modulus (minimal constant) at a reference triple, nor does it treat general \(g\) beyond \(\ell_1\). The results provide structural decompositions and equivalences that could help bound a modulus in the \(\ell_1\) case, but no explicit modulus formula is given.

sources.title: Isolated Calmness in Regularized Convex Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: For the least-squares regularized composite problem \(\min_x \tfrac{1}{2\mu}\|\Phi x-b\|^2+g(Kx)\), the paper gives geometric necessary/sufficient conditions for isolated calmness (local single-valued Lipschitz behavior at an isolated solution) of the solution mapping \(S_{LS}(b,\mu)\). In particular, if \(x\bar\in S_{LS}(\bar b,\bar\mu)\), \(\bar y\in \partial g(K\bar x)\) with \(K^*\bar y=-\tfrac{1}{\bar\mu}\Phi^*(\Phi\bar x-\bar b)\), and \(g\) satisfies quadratic growth at \(K\bar x\) for \(\bar y\), then \(S_{LS}\) is isolated calm at \((\bar b,\bar\mu)\) for \(\bar x\) provided \(\operatorname{Ker}\Phi\cap K^{-1}(T_{\partial g^*(\bar y)}(K\bar x))=\{0\}\); moreover this condition is also necessary under mild qualification assumptions (e.g. \(\partial g^*(\bar y)\) polyhedral or \(\operatorname{Im}K\cap \operatorname{ri}(\partial g^*(\bar y))\neq\emptyset\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computable formulas/estimates of the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) in the regime where it is single-valued and locally Lipschitz, preferably avoiding heavy second-order objects (coderivatives/second-order variational structures of \(g\)). This paper does not compute the Lipschitz modulus. Instead it studies the weaker/related property of isolated calmness (a local Lipschitz bound around an isolated solution) and provides geometric characterizations in terms of tangent cones to \(\partial g^*(\bar y)\) and kernels of \(\Phi\) and \(K^*\). While these conditions can help verify Lipschitz stability and imply existence of some modulus \(\kappa\), the paper does not provide an explicit minimal constant (modulus) nor a computable bound in terms of \(\bar A,\bar b,\bar\mu\) without second-order information; it still uses second-order-related structures (graphical derivatives/second subderivatives) but packages them into tangent-cone conditions under quadratic growth.

sources.title: Nonsmooth Newton methods with effective subspaces for polyhedral regularization
sources.theorem_or_result:
For composite convex problems \(\min_x \phi(x)=f(x)+g(x)\) with \(f\) \(C^2\) and convex and \(g\) convex polyhedral, if \( ar x\) is a tilt-stable minimizer (equivalently, \(-
abla f( ar x)\in \partial g( ar x)\) and \(\ker 
abla^2 f( ar x)\cap \operatorname{par}\partial g^*(-
abla f( ar x))=\{0\}\)), then the proposed Newton method restricted to the effective subspace \(L_k:=\operatorname{par}\partial g^*(z_k)\) is well-defined and the iterates converge quadratically to \( ar x\) (Theorem 3.1; Corollaries 3.3 and 3.8 for ISTA/FISTA-generated \((y_k,z_k)\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computable formulas/estimates of the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) for regularized least squares, avoiding heavy second-order objects (coderivatives of \(\partial g\)). This paper does not compute the Lipschitz modulus of \(S\). However, it develops a first-order-computable object \(\operatorname{par}\partial g^*(z)\) (effective subspace) and uses tilt-stability/metric regularity to obtain quadratic local convergence of Newton-type methods for least-squares-type models with polyhedral regularizers (including Lasso/TV/OSCAR). Tilt-stability is closely tied to strong metric regularity and hence to Lipschitz behavior of solution mappings, so the paper provides algorithmic tools and explicit subspace formulas that could help estimate sensitivity in polyhedral cases, but it does not provide an explicit Lipschitz-modulus formula for \(S(A,b,\mu)\) under general convex \(g\) or even for polyhedral \(g\).

sources.title: Tilt Stability of Ky-Fan -Norm Composite Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper derives point-based necessary and sufficient conditions for tilt stability (single-valued Lipschitz behavior under tilt perturbations) of composite problems of the form \(\min_X\; \nu\,\vartheta(X)+\Psi_\kappa(X)\), where \(\vartheta\) is \(C^2\) and locally convex and \(\Psi_\kappa\) is the Ky-Fan \(\kappa\)-norm. In particular, it provides an explicit formula for the second subderivative \(d^2\Psi_\kappa(X\mid\Gamma)(G)\) (Propositions 3.1–3.2) and a verifiable characterization of tilt stability: a local minimizer \(X\) is tilt-stable iff \(\operatorname{Ker}\nabla^2\vartheta(X)\cap \Upsilon=\{0\}\), where \(\Upsilon\) is an explicitly described set of directions determined by the SVD structure of \((X,\Gamma)\) (Theorem 4.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computable formulas/estimates of the local Lipschitz modulus of the solution mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) for regularized least squares, preferably avoiding heavy second-order variational objects (coderivatives of \(\partial g\)). This paper does not address the parameterized least-squares mapping \(S(A,b,\mu)\) nor compute its Lipschitz modulus. Instead, it studies tilt stability of minimizers for matrix composite problems with Ky-Fan \(\kappa\)-norm regularizers, using explicit second subderivative calculations. Tilt stability implies a single-valued Lipschitz localization of \((\partial f)^{-1}\), so the results provide second-order tools/criteria that are conceptually related to Lipschitz stability, but they rely heavily on second subderivatives and are specialized to spectral regularizers rather than giving modulus formulas for \(S(A,b,\mu)\) in the least-squares setting.
